ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Further concerning volitions and free-wills: minds, too, and knowledges and wisdoms.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: When we say that Christ is perfect God and perfect man, we assuredly attribute to Him all the properties natural to both the Father and mother.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He became man in order that that which was overcome might overcome.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He Who was omnipotent did not in His omnipotent authority and might lack the power to rescue man out of the hands of the tyrant.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But the tyrant would have had a ground of complaint if, after he had overcome man, God should have used force against him.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Wherefore God in His pity and love for man wished to reveal fallen man himself as conqueror, and became man to restore like with like.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But that man is a rational and intelligent animal, no one will deny.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: How, then, could He have become man if He took on Himself flesh without soul, or soul without mind?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For that is not man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, what benefit would His becoming man have been to us if He Who suffered first was not saved, nor renewed and strengthened by the union with divinity?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For that which is not assumed is not remedied.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: He, therefore, assumed the whole man, even the fairest part of him, which had become diseased, in order that He might bestow salvation on the whole.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And, indeed, there could never exist a mind that had not wisdom and was destitute of knowledge.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For if it has not energy or motion, it is utterly reduced to nothingness.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Therefore, God the Word, wishing to restore that which was in His own image, became man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But what is that which was in His own image, unless mind?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: So He gave up the better and assumed the worse.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For mind is in the border-land between God and flesh, for it dwells indeed in fellowship with the flesh, and is, moreover, the image of God.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Mind, then, mingles with mind, and mind holds a place midway between the pureness of God and the denseness of flesh.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For if the Lord assumed a soul without mind, He assumed the soul of an irrational animal.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if the Evangelist said that the Word was made flesh, note that in the Holy Scripture sometimes a man is spoken of as a soul, as, for example, with seventy-five souls came Jacob into Egypt: and sometimes a man is spoken of as flesh, as, for example, All flesh shall see the salvation of God.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And accordingly the Lord did not become flesh without soul or mind, but man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: He says, indeed, Himself, Why seek ye to kill Me, a Man that hath told you the truth?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: He, therefore, assumed flesh animated with the spirit of reason and mind, a spirit that holds sway over the flesh but is itself under the dominion of the divinity of the Word.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: So, then, He had by nature, both as God and as man, the power of will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But His human will was obedient and subordinate to His divine will, not being guided by its own inclination, but willing those things which the divine will willed.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it was with the permission of the divine will that He suffered by nature what was proper to Him.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For when He prayed that He might escape the death, it was with His divine will naturally willing and permitting it that He did so pray and agonize and fear, and again when His divine will willed that His human will should choose the death, the passion became voluntary to Him.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it was not as God only, but also as man, that He voluntarily surrendered Himself to the death.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And thus He bestowed on us also courage in the face of death.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: So, indeed, He said before His saving passion, Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from Me,” manifestly as though He were to drink the cup as man and not as God.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It was as man, then, that He wished the cup to pass from Him: but these are the words of natural timidity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Nevertheless, He said, not My will, that is to say, not in so far as I am of a different essence from Thee, but Thy will be done, that is to say, My will and Thy will, in so far as I am of the same essence as Thou.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Now these are the words of a brave heart.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the Spirit of the Lord, since He truly became man in His good pleasure, on first testing its natural weakness was sensible of the natural fellow-suffering involved in its separation from the body, but being strengthened by the divine will it again grew bold in the face of death.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For since He was Himself wholly God although also man, and wholly man although also God, He Himself as man subjected in Himself and by Himself His human nature to God and the Father, and became obedient to the Father, thus making Himself the most excellent type and example for us.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Of His own free-will, moreover, He exercised His divine and human will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For free-will is assuredly implanted in every rational nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For to what end would it possess reason, if it could not reason at its own free-will?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the Creator hath implanted even in the unreasoning brutes natural appetite to compel them to sustain their own nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For devoid of reason, as they are, they cannot guide their natural appetite but are guided by it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And so, as soon as the appetite for anything has sprung up, straightway arises also the impulse for action.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And thus they do not win praise or happiness for pursuing virtue, nor punishment for doing evil.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But the rational nature, although it does possess a natural appetite, can guide and train it by reason wherever the laws of nature are observed.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the advantage of reason consists in this, the free-will, by which we mean natural activity in a rational subject.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Wherefore in pursuing virtue it wins praise and happiness, and in pursuing vice it wins punishment.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: So that the soul of the Lord being moved of its own free-will willed, but willed of its free-will those things which His divine will willed it to will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the flesh was not moved at a sign from the Word, as Moses and all the holy men were moved at a sign from heaven.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But He Himself, Who was one and yet both God and man, willed according to both His divine and His human will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Wherefore it was not in inclination but rather in natural power that the two wills of the Lord differed from one another.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For His divine will was without beginning and all-effecting, as having power that kept pace with it, and free from passion; while His human will had a beginning in time, and itself endured the natural and innocent passions, and was not naturally omnipotent.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But yet it was omnipotent because it truly and naturally had its origin in the God-Word.
